title: Archangel Michael, St. Demetrius, St. Theophanes the Confessor & St. Gregory the Great
primary_saint: The Archangel Michael, St. Demetrius of Thessalonica, St. Theophanes the Confessor, and St. Gregory the Great Dialogos
narrative:
On the twelfth of Megabit, the monthly feast of the Archangel Michael fills the Church with the awareness of the heavenly intercession that sustains the Lenten community through its fast. Michael's monthly commemoration in the twelfth day is the calendar's way of ensuring that no month passes without the faithful renewing their awareness of the angelic protection that surrounds them — the warrior of Heaven who stands between the human community and the spiritual forces that exploit the vulnerability that fasting and self-examination can produce. In the Lenten season, when the community is deliberately weakened in body in order to be strengthened in spirit, the Archangel's intercession is more urgently sought than in ordinary time, and the churches that bear his name throughout Ethiopia are full on this day with the prayers of those who know their own fragility.

Saint Demetrius of Thessalonica, the beloved patron of that great city of Macedonia, brings to this month's assembly the specific grace of the soldier-martyr in his intercessory aspect. Where Michael is the archangelic warrior, Demetrius is the human martyr whose power did not cease at death but became, through his martyrdom, more rather than less available to those who invoke him. The tradition records an extraordinary number of miraculous interventions attributed to Demetrius on behalf of his city — appearances on the city walls during sieges, healings in his basilica, deliverances from plague — and the Ethiopian tradition honours this continuing activity of the martyr as a sign of the truth that the saints who have passed through death are still fully present to and active in the community they died for.

Saint Theophanes the Confessor is this day's figure of monastic scholarship under imperial pressure: a Byzantine monk and chronicler who was imprisoned by the Emperor Leo the Armenian for his defence of the veneration of sacred images during the second phase of the Iconoclast controversy, and who died of the effects of his imprisonment not long after his release. His Chronicle, covering the history of the Church and Empire from Diocletian to his own time, is one of the primary historical sources for a period of considerable obscurity, and his willingness to maintain it and circulate it under conditions of active imperial hostility makes him one of those scholars whose intellectual courage is no less costly than the martyr's physical courage. The Ethiopian tradition honours him as a guardian of memory, one who knew that the falsification of the past was a form of spiritual violence.

Saint Gregory the Great, called Dialogos — the Writer of Dialogues — in the Eastern tradition, brings this day's assembly to its close with the voice of the Western patriarchate at the height of its pastoral creativity. Gregory's Dialogues, which collected the miraculous lives of Italian saints and established the hagiographical genre in the Western church, his Pastoral Rule which defined the theology of episcopal ministry for the medieval West, and his liturgical reforms which shaped the Roman Rite for more than a millennium — all these achievements are received by the Ethiopian tradition not as the productions of a foreign church but as contributions to the universal Church's treasury. His famous response when asked why he was weeping during the liturgy — that he was weeping because he had just celebrated a mystery about which he understood so little — is treasured in the Ethiopian monastic tradition as the truest description of the posture of the celebrant before the altar. Amen.
